Title: Obesity, Metabolic Inflammation, and Respiratory Comorbidity

Abstract:
Obesity causes a chronic low-grade inflammatory state (metabolic inflammation) that
interacts with environmental exposures to worsen respiratory outcomes. This study
examines causal pathways connecting BMI, adipose inflammation, and respiratory disease.

Introduction:
The obesity epidemic has substantially increased the population-level burden of
inflammatory disease. Adipose tissue in obese individuals is not metabolically inert —
it causes chronic systemic inflammation through adipokine secretion and macrophage
infiltration. This metabolic inflammation likely interacts with other inflammatory
triggers, including air pollution.

Key Causal Findings:
1. Excess adipose tissue causes elevated leptin and reduced adiponectin, shifting
   immune balance toward pro-inflammatory states.

2. Adipose inflammation causes insulin resistance through TNF-α mediated disruption
   of insulin signaling, establishing a causal pathway from obesity to metabolic syndrome.

3. Metabolic inflammation causes lung function impairment independent of smoking and
   pollution, with each 5-unit increase in BMI causing a 1.8% reduction in FEV1/FVC.

4. Obese individuals showed a synergistic response to air pollution: the same PM2.5
   exposure caused 40% greater inflammatory elevation in obese vs. normal-weight individuals.

5. Weight loss (≥10% body weight) caused measurable reductions in IL-6, TNF-α, and
   CRP within 6 months, confirming the causal direction from obesity to inflammation.

Discussion:
The causal pathway obesity → adipose inflammation → systemic inflammation → respiratory
disease is well-supported. However, whether interventions targeting adipose inflammation
directly (without weight loss) can interrupt this pathway is unknown.

The interaction between air pollution-induced and obesity-induced inflammation suggests
shared downstream pathways. Whether these pathways are additive or multiplicative has
implications for identifying the highest-risk populations.

Physical activity may address both causal drivers simultaneously: exercise reduces
both pollution-induced inflammation and obesity-related adipose inflammation. The
combined effect of exercise on both causal pathways to respiratory disease is a
priority area for future research.

Conclusion:
Obesity is an independent causal driver of systemic inflammation and respiratory disease.
The synergistic interaction with air pollution creates compounding risk in obese urban
populations. Causal interventions targeting adipose inflammation offer a promising
therapeutic approach.
